product_target.statement: Product identity for this planning run is contested and not human-resolved. Competing framings are: (A) Moria as a reusable GPU-resident voxel-world substrate delivered as a Rust crate or small crate family, with any walkable-world executable only a public-API validation harness and not a game layer; and (B) Product One as “The Walkable World,” a product-shaped demo of substrate slice + one generated region + third-person character with dig/place proof, performance targets, and milestone demos. Across sources the full game, System/LLM, spells, gas, combat, AI, and building layers are out of scope for this repository. Full architecture in the substrate design spec exceeds Product One and is not uniformly claimed as the current deliverable.

documents.notes: Top-level orientation. Aligns with project-boundary substrate/harness framing. Points at docs/seeds/ as preserved substrate inputs. Not a self-authenticating authority root.

Seed-bundle index preserved from 2026-07-13 source material. Asserts which seeds are “binding” and which are architecture reference; those assertions are evidence for authority decisions, not self-authenticating. Included because root README references docs/seeds/ and this file is the package index there.

Defines Moria’s product as the reusable substrate crate(s) and reclassifies the walkable-world executable as validation harness only. Seeds README labels this the operator’s binding clarification, but that label is not human-confirmed for this run.

Defines Product One “The Walkable World” as a product-shaped demo (world, player, dig proof, performance targets, milestones). Seeds README labels it the binding substrate implementation and harness scope; the document’s own product language competes with the harness-only framing.

Substrate architecture design specification. Product One and seeds README treat it as the architecture reference to select from rather than the sole current-product definition. Companion system-substrate-pivot.md is missing.

claims.statement: Moria is a reusable, GPU-resident voxel-world substrate intended to be consumed by the actual game as a Rust crate.
Its walkable-world executable is a separate consumer and validation harness for terrain generation, streaming, meshing, editing, collision, persistence, and performance—not a game layer.
The substrate inputs are preserved in docs/seeds/.

product-one-seed.md defines the binding substrate implementation and its walkable-world validation harness; voxel-world-substrate.md is the substrate architecture reference and only portions selected by the Product One seed are required for this milestone; project-boundary.md records the operator's binding clarification.
Moria is only the voxel-world substrate. Broader game, System, LLM, spell, gas, combat, AI, and building intent is deliberately absent and out of scope.
These documents are preserved publicly and verbatim from the source material provided on 2026-07-13.
Moria's product is the reusable voxel-world substrate, exposed as a Rust crate or a small family of tightly scoped Rust crates.
The actual game is a separate downstream consumer and is not part of this repository.

Moria may include a walkable-world executable, but that executable is only a validation harness. It must consume the substrate through the same public interfaces available to an external game rather than owning privileged or game-specific implementation paths.

This is an immediate, concrete reason to use a Cargo workspace boundary between the reusable substrate and its validation harness. The precise crate split is a technical-design decision; the consumer boundary is not optional.

Game rules and the future System, LLM, spell, gas, combat, AI, and building layers are out of scope. Compatibility seams may be designed where the substrate requirements demand them, but those layers must not be implemented here.

Scope: the voxel substrate, one generated region, and a character who can run around in it. No System, no spells, no gas, no game. This is the tech proven as a product-shaped demo. Everything here references voxel-world-substrate.md; this doc pins down what gets built first, what the seed world contains, and what "done" looks like.

A generated natural world — hills, forest, river, cliffs, caves — that you can run through in third person, where everything you see is voxel truth: you can walk to any point, the terrain is continuous and smooth, and a debug key proves it's all mutable matter underneath. The demo's job is to make one claim undeniable: this is not a heightmap with props — it's a fully material world, and it looks good.

Non-goals (explicitly out): combat, stats, entities beyond the player, AI; The System / LLM; gas, pricing, intent; building UI, blueprints, mechanisms; fluids beyond static bodies (tier 1 only); weather sim, seasons, growth (fixed time-of-day slider enough); persistence beyond reload same seed + deltas (single save slot, no versioning).
Dig and place stay in scope, on a debug key. Not as gameplay — as the proof that the world is mutable matter rather than a Unity-style terrain scene.
One authored seed for a 1km × 1km × 256m region at 25cm voxels / 16³ bricks; the whole region must not fit in memory as raw voxels (streaming/sparsity test).
Seed world must produce rolling meadow, dense mixed forest, river + lake, cliff/strata outcrop, karst cave to ~-40m, aquifer band and ore vein, micro voxel objects, and a small ruin stamp.

locator.heading: Terrain composition (what the generator must produce from this seed)

claims.statement: Materials palette seed set (~14): air, water, topsoil, subsoil, sand, gravel, limestone, sandstone, shale, granite, iron-ore, wood, leaf, cut-stone; state nibble exists but no CA consumes it in product one.

Product one builds generation layer full (continent pass may be stubbed to curated region parameters); matter layer partial (brick pool, meshing, grass/clutter, voxel objects placement/render yes; felling/rigid no; CA/fire/fluids-tier-2/3/integrity/granular no; static water tier 1 yes); script/API layer sliver (dig/place verbs and mirror queries as engine-internal API; no embedded scripting language yet).

Third-person character controller (run, sprint, jump, swim surface paddle) with collision against voxel truth not render mesh; free-orbit third-person camera; continuous cliff-top to -40m cave route; debug dig/place and visualization keys.

Performance targets: 60fps at 1440p on 3060-class and on M4 Mac Mini; dig-to-remesh within 2 frames for 3m-radius carve; cold-start < 5s to walkable; full region under ~2GB GPU resident; delta save < 50MB after heavy defacement; benchmarks with machine profile are part of the deliverable.

Dev-platform constraints: no 64-bit buffer atomics; bandwidth is the ceiling; keep FleX command/mirror architecture; stay on wgpu/WGSL with no native Metal fork in load-bearing layers; discrete-GPU targets provisional until Linux box returns.

locator.heading: Dev-platform constraints (M4 Mac Mini, 32GB unified, wgpu/Metal)

claims.statement: Milestones in order: hill looks like a hill; carve smooth tunnel; true geology; dressed world; the run (controller+camera+route); numbers (streaming, persistence, benchmarks); stretch timber felling.

What Product One buys: substrate crates exist, benchmarked, API boundary enforced; public X-audience artifacts and downloadable demo at milestone 5; decision bed for open substrate questions via measurements; Product two (fortress-mode toybox or ARPG with System) starts from a walkable world.
This doc is companion to system-substrate-pivot.md and builds on GPU-resident brick pool, CA sim, and FleX-pattern coupling from the pivot doc without relitigating that design.

Design goals: reads as a normal world; mutable everywhere all the way down; deep Z first-class; substrate not game (same crate stack supports System ARPG, DF-style fortress, Moria-style descent, or pure sandbox); GPU-resident.
Substrate, not game. Clean layering so the same crate stack supports multiple game modes; game rules live above the substrate; the substrate provides matter, physics, queries, and mutation.
The System (LLM) hooks are noted where they attach, but this substrate must stand alone as an engine layer with zero LLM dependency.

Recommended look strategy is hybrid: smooth isosurface extraction (surface nets or dual contouring) for terrain/structures; vegetation and clutter as voxel-backed objects or instanced dressing, not raw cube aesthetic as primary.

locator.heading: 2. The Look Problem: Voxels That Don't Look Like Voxels

claims.statement: Voxel size for the world grid: 25cm (4 voxels/meter); 16³ bricks (4m cubes at 25cm); homogeneous sentinels for empty/full bricks.

locator.heading: 2. The Look Problem: Voxels That Don't Look Like Voxels

claims.statement: Storage model: brick pool with homogeneous sentinels; per-voxel material/density/state packing; per-brick aggregates; 2D column index for surface height and DF-style Z views; lazy materialization from worldgen.
World generation is geology-first (continent/climate, terrain, strata, cave/void passes) with lazy materialization; System attaches only to metadata/placement/palettes, not geology noise.
Vegetation model: trees and micro objects as voxel objects (felling/rigid conversion for trees); grass/clutter as instanced dressing from scatter points; granular materials get settle CA.
Fluids use three tiers: static bodies, coarse brick-aggregate flow, and fine PBD splash particles.

Architecture also specifies weather/ambient sim, structural integrity/cave-ins, building placement/blueprints/mechanisms/rooms, entities/pathfinding/Z-axis nav, and persistence/streaming rings — supporting future fortress/ARPG consumers beyond Product One.

Layering as reusable crates: game layer above semantic, script/API, matter, and generation layers; gas is a policy object; System is a game-layer client; nothing above the matter layer touches voxels directly.

locator.heading: 12. Layering: The Substrate as Reusable Crates

claims.statement: Substrate vertical-slice build order includes brick pool through persistence/streaming, with milestones that go beyond Product One (brick CA fire, tree felling, fluids tier 2, integrity/cave-ins).

locator.heading: 13. Build Order (vertical slice for the substrate itself)

claims.statement: Open questions include final voxel size (25cm vs 12.5cm), distant LOD strategy, object-layer capacity/spatial acceleration, fluids tier-2 pressure solve vs momentum-only, and multiplayer scope statements.
Product two (fortress-mode toybox or the ARPG with the System) starts from a walkable world instead of a whiteboard.
Downstream game clients contemplated by the substrate include System ARPG, DF-style fortress/colony, Moria-style descent roguelike, and pure sandbox; they are not this repository's product.

decisions.question: What is the product identity / product target for Moria in this planning run?

options.label: Reusable voxel-world substrate crate(s)
options.summary: Moria’s product is the reusable GPU-resident voxel-world substrate exposed as a Rust crate or small crate family. Any walkable-world executable is consumer/harness only, not the product itself.

options.label: Product One walkable-world demo
options.summary: The current product is Product One “The Walkable World”: a product-shaped demo of generated region + third-person character + dig/place proof, which also creates the substrate crates as an outcome.

options.label: Full substrate architecture scope
options.summary: The product target is the full engine-layer substrate described in voxel-world-substrate.md (including systems beyond Product One’s slice).

decisions.rationale: Root README and project-boundary state the product is the reusable substrate crate with harness-only walkable world. Product One states a product-shaped walkable-world demo as Product Statement and titles the seed “The Walkable World.” Seeds README tries to merge these by calling Product One the binding implementation of substrate plus validation harness, but that merge is an index assertion, not human resolution. No human feedback selects among these framings.

decisions.question: Which seed documents are binding for product identity and current-deliverable scope versus architecture reference or context only?

options.label: Seeds README stack
options.summary: Treat project-boundary as binding product clarification, product-one-seed as binding milestone/implementation scope, voxel-world-substrate as architecture reference subsetted by Product One, and root/seeds README as context — as docs/seeds/README.md asserts.

options.label: Project boundary primary
options.summary: Treat project-boundary (with root README) as binding product identity; treat product-one as supporting demo/harness content only; treat voxel-world-substrate as non-binding architecture reference.

options.label: Product One primary
options.summary: Treat product-one-seed as the binding product and done-definition; treat project-boundary and substrate as supporting constraints/reference.

options.label: Joint boundary + Product One
options.summary: Treat project-boundary as binding for repository/product identity and consumer boundary, and product-one-seed as binding for the current vertical-slice content of substrate + harness demo, with voxel-world-substrate as selectable architecture reference.

decisions.rationale: Seeds README explicitly assigns “binding” roles, but filename, ordering, and the word “binding” are not self-authenticating. Product One and project-boundary each read as definitional for different product framings. No human feedback confirms a document authority stack.

decisions.question: What is the current deliverable scope for this planning run?

options.label: Substrate crates + public-API harness shell
options.summary: Deliver reusable substrate crate(s) and a walkable validation harness that only uses public APIs; detailed Product One world/player content is optional or secondary unless separately selected.

options.label: Product One vertical slice
options.summary: Deliver the Product One slice: generation full (region-curated), matter partial, script/API sliver, player/camera/debug tools, seed-world features, performance targets, and milestones 1–6 (timber stretch optional).

options.label: Full substrate build-order scope
options.summary: Deliver through the broader voxel-world-substrate build order (including CA/fire, fluids tier 2, integrity/cave-ins, and related systems beyond Product One non-goals).

decisions.rationale: Project-boundary emphasizes crate + harness boundary and excludes game layers. Product One specifies a concrete world/player/performance milestone plan. The architecture doc’s build order and systems are wider than Product One’s non-goals. These are mutually exclusive current-scope choices without human selection.

decisions.question: What is the authority role of the walkable-world executable relative to the substrate?

options.label: Validation harness only
options.summary: Walkable-world is solely a public-API validation harness and consumer of the substrate; it must not own privileged game-specific paths and is not itself the product.

options.label: Primary demo product
options.summary: Walkable-world is the Product One product-shaped demo deliverable (playable character route, postcard world, downloadable demo), for which the substrate is the underlying tech.

options.label: Harness whose content follows Product One
options.summary: Walkable-world remains a validation harness architecturally (public API only), but its required content and acceptance bar are defined by Product One’s world, player, and performance milestones.

decisions.rationale: README and project-boundary repeatedly call the executable a validation harness, not a game layer. Product One describes it as a product-shaped demo with audience/downloadable-demo outcomes. Seeds README merges both labels. Human resolution is required to pick the operative role.

deferred_design_questions.question: Final voxel size: keep 25cm, move to 12.5cm, or allow per-region variable resolution?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Both Product One and the substrate assume 25cm for now; Product One says the region is the benchmark bed for the 25cm vs 12.5cm question. This is a measurement-driven technical choice, not a product-identity gate.

deferred_design_questions.question: How should distant terrain be represented: chunked marching-cubes LOD versus imposter cards from the column index?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Open architecture question in the substrate spec; does not change product identity or whether current deliverable is crate vs walkable demo.

deferred_design_questions.question: At what tree/object count does the object registry need its own spatial acceleration structure?

options.label: Defer until measured under Product One forest density
Ship spatial acceleration from the start

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Scaling/implementation detail for the object layer; Product One includes forest density as a stress case for later measurement.

deferred_design_questions.question: If/when fluids tier 2 is built, does it need a pressure solve (DF-accurate U-bend) or is momentum-only enough?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Product One excludes fluids beyond static tier-1 bodies. Solver choice is future architecture when tier 2 is in scope.

deferred_design_questions.question: What is the precise Cargo crate split inside the workspace boundary between reusable substrate and validation harness?

options.label: Family of tightly scoped crates matching layering diagram

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Project-boundary explicitly marks the consumer/workspace boundary as required and the precise crate split as a technical-design decision.

deferred_design_questions.question: Should multiplayer/server-authoritative readiness remain in scope statements even if multiplayer is not built?

options.label: Keep multiplayer-ready verb architecture in scope statements
Omit multiplayer from scope statements until a later product

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Architecture note only; does not decide current product identity or deliverable content.

deferred_design_questions.question: Primary terrain meshing algorithm: surface nets, dual contouring, or hybrid/material-dependent extraction?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Both docs mention surface nets/dual contouring as the smooth-extraction approach; exact primary algorithm is an implementation choice after authority is set.

deferred_design_questions.question: How should planning treat GPU-resident / FleX coupling details that the substrate doc defers to the missing system-substrate-pivot.md?

options.label: Rely on inline echoes in seeds (wgpu, FleX mirror, brick pool) without the pivot
Recover or re-author system-substrate-pivot.md before detailed GPU design

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Missing companion is a provenance gap. Enough product/GPU constraints appear in README and Product One that product identity is still decidable; recovering pivot content is a design-input completeness issue, not an authority choice among present product targets.
